'''Tatami''' (畳) are types of [[:en:Mat|Mat]] used as flooring material in traditional [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese-style rooms. They are made in standard sizes, twice as long as wide, about 0.9 metres (3') by 1.8 metres (6') depending on the region. In martial arts, tatami are the floor used for training in a [[:en:Dojo|Dojo]] and for competition.<ref>{{Cite web|title=The Quest for the Perfect Judo Floor {{!}} Judo Info|url=https://judoinfo.com/tatami/|website=judoinfo.com|access-date=2020-05-05}}</ref>
Tatami are covered with a [[:en:Weft|Weft]]-faced weave of {{nihongo|[[:en:Juncus effusus|soft rush]]|藺草|igusa}} ([[:en:common rush|common rush]]), on a warp of hemp or weaker cotton. There are four warps per weft [[:en:shed (weaving)|shed]], two at each end (or sometimes two per shed, one at each end, to cut costs). The {{transl|ja|doko}} (core) is traditionally made from sewn-together rice straw, but contemporary tatami sometimes have [[:en:chip board|compressed wood chip boards]] or [[:en:extruded polystyrene foam|extruded polystyrene foam]] in their cores, instead or as well. The long sides are usually {{nihongo|edged|縁|heri}} with [[:en:Brocade|Brocade]] or plain cloth, although some tatami have no edging.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.kyo-tatami.com/english/about|title=Understanding Tatami|date=28 June 2015|access-date=2016-10-31 |publisher=[[Motoyama Tatami shop]] }}</ref><ref name="igusa">{{cite web |title=Structure of Tatami |url=https://kyo-tatami.com/world/about/ |website=kyo-tatami.com |publisher=Motoyama Tatami Shop |access-date=14 June 2021 |date=2015-06-28}}</ref>
<gallery caption=Construction mode=packed> File:Modern tatami.JPG|Machine-sewing of tatami File:Tatami sectional view.jpg|Cross-section of a modern tatami with an [[:en:extruded polystyrene foam|extruded polystyrene foam]] core File:Men Making Tatami Mats, 1860 - ca. 1900.jpg|Making tatami mats, late 19th century. File:Tatami.jpg|Close-up of mat surface and edging </gallery>
==History==
The term ''tatami'' is derived from the verb {{nihongo||畳む|tatamu}}, meaning 'to fold' or 'to pile'. This indicates that the early tatami were thin and could be folded up when not used or piled in layers.<ref name="KoandaTatami">Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan, entry for "tatami".</ref>
Tatami were originally a luxury item for the nobility. The lower classes had mat-covered earthen floors.<ref name="tatamiluxury">{{cite web |url=http://www.yoshinoantiques.com/Interior-article.html |title=The Yoshino Newsletter |access-date=2007-03-28 |work=Floors/Tatami |publisher=[[Yoshino Japanese Antiques]] |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070331133235/http://www.yoshinoantiques.com/Interior-article.html |archive-date=2007-03-31 }}</ref> During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], when the [[:en:Shinden-zukuri|Shinden-zukuri]] architectural style of aristocratic residences was consummated, the flooring of shinden-zukuri palatial rooms were mainly wooden, and tatami were only used as seating for the highest aristocrats.<ref name="Sato Osamu 1994"/>
In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], there arose the [[:en:Shoin-zukuri|Shoin-zukuri]] architectural style of residence for the samurai and priests who had gained power. This architectural style reached its peak of development in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], when tatami gradually came to be spread over whole rooms, beginning with small rooms. Rooms completely spread with tatami came to be known as {{nihongo||座敷||{{lit.|spread out for sitting}}}}, and rules concerning seating and etiquette determined the arrangement of the tatami in the rooms.<ref name="Sato Osamu 1994"/>
It is said that prior to the mid-16th century, the ruling nobility and samurai slept on tatami or woven mats called {{nihongo||茣蓙|goza}}, while commoners used straw mats or loose straw for bedding.<ref>Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan, entry for "bedding"</ref> Tatami were gradually popularized and reached the homes of commoners toward the end of the 17th century.<ref name="tatamicommoners">{{cite web |url=http://www.kcif.or.jp/archive/en/newsletter/lik/archives/0304/04_2003.htm |title=Kyoto International Community House Newsletter |access-date=2007-03-28 |work=2nd section titled History of tatami |publisher=[[Kyoto City International Foundation]] }}</ref>
Houses built in Japan today often have few tatami-floored rooms, if any. Having just one is not uncommon. The rooms having tatami flooring and other such traditional architectural features are referred to as ''nihonma'' or ''[[:en:Washitsu|Washitsu]]'', "Japanese-style rooms".
<gallery mode="packed" heights="200"> File:Saikū Historical Museum - Display item04 - The palace of Saiô - Miniature model.jpg|Green tatami in a museum model of the [[Saikū]] palace in ~the 9th century File:Gaki zoshi - Tokyo - part 1.jpeg|Courtiers making music, [[:wiktionary:circa#English|circa]] 1150-1200 File:Otomo no Yakamochi Agedatamibon.gif|As a dais, ~13th century File:Tengu zoshi, Nezu Museum scroll, detail 2.jpg|An almost-completely-covered floor in an illustration drawn in 1296 File:Sleeping two, Kasuga Gongen Genki (1309).jpg|Tatami being used as sleeping mats, 1309 (see [[:en:Futon|Futon]]) </gallery>
==Size==
The size of tatami traditionally differs between regions in Japan:
* [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]: {{cvt|0.955|by|1.91|m}}, called {{nihongo||京間|Kyōma}} ''tatami'' * [[:en:Nagoya|Nagoya]]: {{cvt|0.91|by|1.82|m}}, called {{nihongo||中京間|Chūkyōma}} or {{nihongo||合の間|Ainoma|{{lit.}} "in-between" size}} ''tatami'' * [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]: {{cvt|0.88|by|1.76|m}}, called {{nihongo||江戸間|Edoma}} or {{nihongo||関東間|Kantōma}} ''tatami''
In terms of thickness, {{cvt|5.5|cm}} is average for a ''Kyōma'' tatami, while {{cvt|6.0|cm}} is the norm for a ''Kantōma'' tatami.<ref name="Sato Osamu 1994">Sato Osamu, "A History of Tatami," in Chanoyu Quarterly no. 77 (1994).</ref> A half mat is called a {{nihongo||半畳|hanjō}}, and a mat of three-quarter length, which is used in tea-ceremony rooms (''[[:en:Chashitsu|Chashitsu]]''), is called ''daimedatami'' ({{lang|ja|大目畳}} or {{lang|ja|台目畳}}).<ref name="KoandaTatami"/> In terms of [[Japanese units of measurement#Length|traditional Japanese length units]], a tatami is (allowing for regional variation) 1 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'' by 0.5 ''ken'', or equivalently 6 ''[[:en:Shaku (unit)|shaku]]'' by 3 ''shaku'' – formally this is {{cvt|1.81818|×|0.90909|m|in|0}}, the size of Nagoya tatami. Note that a ''shaku'' is almost the same length as one foot in the traditional English-American measurement system.
In Japan, the size of a room is often measured by the number of {{nihongo|''tatami'' mats|-畳|-jō}}, about {{cvt|1.653|m2}} for a standard Nagoya-size tatami. Alternatively, in terms of [[Japanese units of measurement#Area|traditional Japanese area units]], room area (and especially house floor area) is measured in terms of ''[[:en:Tsubo|Tsubo]],'' where one ''tsubo'' is the area of two tatami mats (a square); formally 1 ''ken'' by 1 ''ken'' or about {{cvt|3.306|m2}}.
Some common room sizes in the Nagoya region are: *{{frac|4|1|2}} mats = 9 shaku × 9 shaku ≈ {{cvt|2.73|×|2.73|m}} *6 mats = 9 shaku × 12 shaku ≈ {{cvt|2.73|×|3.64|m}} *8 mats = 12 shaku × 12 shaku ≈ {{cvt|3.64|×|3.64|m}}
Shops were traditionally designed to be {{frac|5|1|2}} mats, and [[:en:Chashitsu|tea rooms]] are frequently {{frac|4|1|2}} mats.{{Citation needed|date=July 2009}}
A special format is the [[:en:Ryūkyū|Ryūkyū]] (琉球) ''tatami'', which is in square-size and can have various measurements.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.ryu-kyutatami.com/f/about-ryukyutatami | title=琉球畳とは }}</ref> The Ryūkyū ''tatami'' does not have a border and is therefore becoming popular in modern rooms for its simplicity.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.ikehikojapan.com/blogs/product/l_20210111 | title=Unexpected advantages and disadvantages of Ryukyu tatami mats in traditional traditional Japanese style rooms | date=20 February 2023 }}</ref>
==Layout== There are rules concerning the number of tatami mats and the layout of the tatami mats in a room. {{anchor|auspicious|inauspicious}} In the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], {{nihongo|"auspicious"|祝儀敷き|shūgijiki}} tatami arrangements and {{nihongo|"inauspicious"|不祝儀敷き|fushūgijiki}} tatami arrangements were distinctly differentiated, and the tatami accordingly would be rearranged depending on the occasion. In modern practice, the "auspicious" layout is ordinarily used. In this arrangement, the junctions of the tatami form a "T" shape; in the "inauspicious" arrangement, the tatami are in a grid pattern wherein the junctions form a "+" shape.<ref name="Sato Osamu 1994"/> An auspicious tiling often requires the use of {{frac|1|2}} mats to tile a room.<ref name="tilings">{{cite conference | last1 = Erickson | first1 = Alejandro | last2 = Ruskey | first2 = FRank | author2-link = FRank Ruskey | last3 = Schurch | first3 = Mark | last4 = Woodcock | first4 = Jennifer | editor1-last = Thai | editor1-first = My T. | editor2-last = Sahni | editor2-first = Sartaj | arxiv = 1103.3309 | contribution = Auspicious tatami mat arrangements | doi = 10.1007/978-3-642-14031-0_32 | mr = 2720105 | pages = 288–297 | publisher = Springer | series = Lecture Notes in Computer Science | title = Computing and Combinatorics, 16th Annual International Conference, COCOON 2010, Nha Trang, Vietnam, July 19-21, 2010. Proceedings | volume = 6196 | year = 2010}}</ref> It is [[:en:NP-complete|NP-complete]] to determine whether a large room has an auspicious arrangement using only full mats.<ref>{{cite conference | last1 = Erickson | first1 = Alejandro | last2 = Ruskey | first2 = FRank | author2-link = FRank Ruskey | editor1-last = Lecroq | editor1-first = Thierry | editor2-last = Mouchard | editor2-first = Laurent | arxiv = 1305.6669 | contribution = Domino tatami covering is NP-complete | doi = 10.1007/978-3-642-45278-9_13 | location = Heidelberg | mr = 3162068 | pages = 140–149 | publisher = Springer | series = Lecture Notes in Computer Science | title = Combinatorial Algorithms: 24th International Workshop, IWOCA 2013, Rouen, France, July 10-12, 2013, Revised Selected Papers | volume = 8288 | year = 2013| s2cid = 12738241 }}</ref>
An inauspicious layout was used to avoid bad fortune at inauspicious events, such as funerals. Now it is widely associated with bad luck and itself avoided.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Kalland|first=Arne|date=April 1999|title=Houses, People and Good Fortune: Geomancy and Vernacular Architecture in Japan|journal=Worldviews|volume=3|issue=1|pages=33–50|jstor=43809122|doi=10.1163/156853599X00036}}</ref> <gallery mode=packed heights=200> File:JapanHomes101 ARRANGEMENT OF MATS IN DIFFERENT-SIZED ROOMS.jpg|upright=0.3|Some auspicious layouts from the early 1800s ([[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]) Image:Tatami layout 1.svg|One possible auspicious layout of a {{frac|4|1|2}} mat room {{legend|#ff7f7f|Half mat}}{{legend|#4992ff|Full mat}} Image:Tearoom layout.svg|Typical layout of a {{frac|4|1|2}} mat tea room in the cold season, when the hearth built into the floor is in use. The room has a ''[[tokonoma]]'' and ''[[Mizuya#The special mizuya dōko|mizuya dōko]]'' Image:Youkoukan06n4592.jpg|Room with tatami flooring in an inauspicious layout and paper doors ([[:en:Shōji|Shōji]]) File:Anforet 3F 2017-06-02 ac (2).jpg|An auspicious layout File:ITatami (8465411258).jpg|"T" shape File:Tatami (51687126569).jpg|Ryūkyū ''tatami'' are square shaped without borders </gallery>
==See also== * [[:en:Higashiyama Bunka|Higashiyama Bunka]] in [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]

'''Tatami''' (畳) are types of [[:en:Mat|Mat]] used as flooring material in traditional [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese-style rooms. They are made in standard sizes, twice as long as wide, about 0.9 metres (3') by 1.8 metres (6') depending on the region. In martial arts, tatami are the floor used for training in a [[:en:Dojo|Dojo]] and for competition.<ref>{{Cite web|title=The Quest for the Perfect Judo Floor {{!}} Judo Info|url=https://judoinfo.com/tatami/|website=judoinfo.com|access-date=2020-05-05}}</ref>
Tatami are covered with a [[:en:Weft|Weft]]-faced weave of {{nihongo|[[:en:Juncus effusus|soft rush]]|藺草|igusa}} ([[:en:common rush|common rush]]), on a warp of hemp or weaker cotton. There are four warps per weft [[:en:shed (weaving)|shed]], two at each end (or sometimes two per shed, one at each end, to cut costs). The {{transl|ja|doko}} (core) is traditionally made from sewn-together rice straw, but contemporary tatami sometimes have [[:en:chip board|compressed wood chip boards]] or [[:en:extruded polystyrene foam|extruded polystyrene foam]] in their cores, instead or as well. The long sides are usually {{nihongo|edged|縁|heri}} with [[:en:Brocade|Brocade]] or plain cloth, although some tatami have no edging.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.kyo-tatami.com/english/about|title=Understanding Tatami|date=28 June 2015|access-date=2016-10-31 |publisher=[[Motoyama Tatami shop]] }}</ref><ref name="igusa">{{cite web |title=Structure of Tatami |url=https://kyo-tatami.com/world/about/ |website=kyo-tatami.com |publisher=Motoyama Tatami Shop |access-date=14 June 2021 |date=2015-06-28}}</ref>

File:Tatami.jpg|Close-up of mat surface and edging </gallery>
==History==
The term ''tatami'' is derived from the verb {{nihongo||畳む|tatamu}}, meaning 'to fold' or 'to pile'. This indicates that the early tatami were thin and could be folded up when not used or piled in layers.<ref name="KoandaTatami">Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan, entry for "tatami".</ref>
Tatami were originally a luxury item for the nobility. The lower classes had mat-covered earthen floors.<ref name="tatamiluxury">{{cite web |url=http://www.yoshinoantiques.com/Interior-article.html |title=The Yoshino Newsletter |access-date=2007-03-28 |work=Floors/Tatami |publisher=[[Yoshino Japanese Antiques]] |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070331133235/http://www.yoshinoantiques.com/Interior-article.html |archive-date=2007-03-31 }}</ref> During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], when the [[:en:Shinden-zukuri|Shinden-zukuri]] architectural style of aristocratic residences was consummated, the flooring of shinden-zukuri palatial rooms were mainly wooden, and tatami were only used as seating for the highest aristocrats.<ref name="Sato Osamu 1994"/>
In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], there arose the [[:en:Shoin-zukuri|Shoin-zukuri]] architectural style of residence for the samurai and priests who had gained power. This architectural style reached its peak of development in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], when tatami gradually came to be spread over whole rooms, beginning with small rooms. Rooms completely spread with tatami came to be known as {{nihongo||座敷||{{lit.|spread out for sitting}}}}, and rules concerning seating and etiquette determined the arrangement of the tatami in the rooms.<ref name="Sato Osamu 1994"/>
It is said that prior to the mid-16th century, the ruling nobility and samurai slept on tatami or woven mats called {{nihongo||茣蓙|goza}}, while commoners used straw mats or loose straw for bedding.<ref>Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan, entry for "bedding"</ref> Tatami were gradually popularized and reached the homes of commoners toward the end of the 17th century.<ref name="tatamicommoners">{{cite web |url=http://www.kcif.or.jp/archive/en/newsletter/lik/archives/0304/04_2003.htm |title=Kyoto International Community House Newsletter |access-date=2007-03-28 |work=2nd section titled History of tatami |publisher=[[Kyoto City International Foundation]] }}</ref>

==Size==
The size of tatami traditionally differs between regions in Japan:
* [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]: {{cvt|0.955|by|1.91|m}}, called {{nihongo||京間|Kyōma}} ''tatami'' * [[:en:Nagoya|Nagoya]]: {{cvt|0.91|by|1.82|m}}, called {{nihongo||中京間|Chūkyōma}} or {{nihongo||合の間|Ainoma|{{lit.}} "in-between" size}} ''tatami'' * [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]: {{cvt|0.88|by|1.76|m}}, called {{nihongo||江戸間|Edoma}} or {{nihongo||関東間|Kantōma}} ''tatami''
In terms of thickness, {{cvt|5.5|cm}} is average for a ''Kyōma'' tatami, while {{cvt|6.0|cm}} is the norm for a ''Kantōma'' tatami.<ref name="Sato Osamu 1994">Sato Osamu, "A History of Tatami," in Chanoyu Quarterly no. 77 (1994).</ref> A half mat is called a {{nihongo||半畳|hanjō}}, and a mat of three-quarter length, which is used in tea-ceremony rooms (''[[:en:Chashitsu|Chashitsu]]''), is called ''daimedatami'' ({{lang|ja|大目畳}} or {{lang|ja|台目畳}}).<ref name="KoandaTatami"/> In terms of [[Japanese units of measurement#Length|traditional Japanese length units]], a tatami is (allowing for regional variation) 1 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'' by 0.5 ''ken'', or equivalently 6 ''[[:en:Shaku (unit)|shaku]]'' by 3 ''shaku'' – formally this is {{cvt|1.81818|×|0.90909|m|in|0}}, the size of Nagoya tatami. Note that a ''shaku'' is almost the same length as one foot in the traditional English-American measurement system.
In Japan, the size of a room is often measured by the number of {{nihongo|''tatami'' mats|-畳|-jō}}, about {{cvt|1.653|m2}} for a standard Nagoya-size tatami. Alternatively, in terms of [[Japanese units of measurement#Area|traditional Japanese area units]], room area (and especially house floor area) is measured in terms of ''[[:en:Tsubo|Tsubo]],'' where one ''tsubo'' is the area of two tatami mats (a square); formally 1 ''ken'' by 1 ''ken'' or about {{cvt|3.306|m2}}.
Some common room sizes in the Nagoya region are: *{{frac|4|1|2}} mats = 9 shaku × 9 shaku ≈ {{cvt|2.73|×|2.73|m}} *6 mats = 9 shaku × 12 shaku ≈ {{cvt|2.73|×|3.64|m}} *8 mats = 12 shaku × 12 shaku ≈ {{cvt|3.64|×|3.64|m}}
Shops were traditionally designed to be {{frac|5|1|2}} mats, and [[:en:Chashitsu|tea rooms]] are frequently {{frac|4|1|2}} mats.{{Citation needed|date=July 2009}}
A special format is the [[:en:Ryūkyū|Ryūkyū]] (琉球) ''tatami'', which is in square-size and can have various measurements.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.ryu-kyutatami.com/f/about-ryukyutatami | title=琉球畳とは }}</ref> The Ryūkyū ''tatami'' does not have a border and is therefore becoming popular in modern rooms for its simplicity.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.ikehikojapan.com/blogs/product/l_20210111 | title=Unexpected advantages and disadvantages of Ryukyu tatami mats in traditional traditional Japanese style rooms | date=20 February 2023 }}</ref>
==Layout== There are rules concerning the number of tatami mats and the layout of the tatami mats in a room. {{anchor|auspicious|inauspicious}} In the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], {{nihongo|"auspicious"|祝儀敷き|shūgijiki}} tatami arrangements and {{nihongo|"inauspicious"|不祝儀敷き|fushūgijiki}} tatami arrangements were distinctly differentiated, and the tatami accordingly would be rearranged depending on the occasion. In modern practice, the "auspicious" layout is ordinarily used. In this arrangement, the junctions of the tatami form a "T" shape; in the "inauspicious" arrangement, the tatami are in a grid pattern wherein the junctions form a "+" shape.<ref name="Sato Osamu 1994"/> An auspicious tiling often requires the use of {{frac|1|2}} mats to tile a room.<ref name="tilings">{{cite conference | last1 = Erickson | first1 = Alejandro | last2 = Ruskey | first2 = FRank | author2-link = FRank Ruskey | last3 = Schurch | first3 = Mark | last4 = Woodcock | first4 = Jennifer | editor1-last = Thai | editor1-first = My T. | editor2-last = Sahni | editor2-first = Sartaj | arxiv = 1103.3309 | contribution = Auspicious tatami mat arrangements | doi = 10.1007/978-3-642-14031-0_32 | mr = 2720105 | pages = 288–297 | publisher = Springer | series = Lecture Notes in Computer Science | title = Computing and Combinatorics, 16th Annual International Conference, COCOON 2010, Nha Trang, Vietnam, July 19-21, 2010. Proceedings | volume = 6196 | year = 2010}}</ref> It is [[:en:NP-complete|NP-complete]] to determine whether a large room has an auspicious arrangement using only full mats.<ref>{{cite conference | last1 = Erickson | first1 = Alejandro | last2 = Ruskey | first2 = FRank | author2-link = FRank Ruskey | editor1-last = Lecroq | editor1-first = Thierry | editor2-last = Mouchard | editor2-first = Laurent | arxiv = 1305.6669 | contribution = Domino tatami covering is NP-complete | doi = 10.1007/978-3-642-45278-9_13 | location = Heidelberg | mr = 3162068 | pages = 140–149 | publisher = Springer | series = Lecture Notes in Computer Science | title = Combinatorial Algorithms: 24th International Workshop, IWOCA 2013, Rouen, France, July 10-12, 2013, Revised Selected Papers | volume = 8288 | year = 2013| s2cid = 12738241 }}</ref>
An inauspicious layout was used to avoid bad fortune at inauspicious events, such as funerals. Now it is widely associated with bad luck and itself avoided.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Kalland|first=Arne|date=April 1999|title=Houses, People and Good Fortune: Geomancy and Vernacular Architecture in Japan|journal=Worldviews|volume=3|issue=1|pages=33–50|jstor=43809122|doi=10.1163/156853599X00036}}</ref> <gallery mode=packed heights=200> File:JapanHomes101 ARRANGEMENT OF MATS IN DIFFERENT-SIZED ROOMS.jpg|upright=0.3|Some auspicious layouts from the early 1800s ([[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]) Image:Tatami layout 1.svg|One possible auspicious layout of a {{frac|4|1|2}} mat room {{legend|#ff7f7f|Half mat}}{{legend|#4992ff|Full mat}} Image:Tearoom layout.svg|Typical layout of a {{frac|4|1|2}} mat tea room in the cold season, when the hearth built into the floor is in use. The room has a ''[[tokonoma]]'' and ''[[Mizuya#The special mizuya dōko|mizuya dōko]]'' Image:Youkoukan06n4592.jpg|Room with tatami flooring in an inauspicious layout and paper doors ([[:en:Shōji|Shōji]]) File:Anforet 3F 2017-06-02 ac (2).jpg|An auspicious layout File:ITatami (8465411258).jpg|"T" shape File:Tatami (51687126569).jpg|Ryūkyū ''tatami'' are square shaped without borders </gallery>
==See also== * [[:en:Higashiyama Bunka|Higashiyama Bunka]] in [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]

(حصيرة)]][[ast:Tatami]][[az:Tatami]][[bcl:Tatami]][[bg:Татами]][[ca:Tatami]][[cs:Tatami]][[cy:Tatami]][[da:Tatami]][[de:Tatami]][[en:Tatami]][[eo:Tatamo]][[es:Tatami]][[et:Tatami]][[eu:Tatami]][[fa:تاتامی]][[fi:Tatami]][[fr:Tatami]][[he:טטאמי]][[hi:तातामी]][[hr:Tatami]][[hu:Tatami]][[hy:Տատամի]][[id:Tatami]][[it:Tatami]][[ja:畳]][[kab:Agertil]][[ko:다다미]][[mk:Татами]][[ms:Tatami]][[nl:Tatami]][[no:Tatami]][[pl:Tatami]][[pt:Tatame]][[ro:Tatami]][[ru:Татами]][[sh:Tatami]][[simple:Tatami]][[sl:Tatami]][[sv:Tatami]][[tr:Tatami]][[uk:Татамі]][[vi:Tatami]][[zh-classical:疊]][[zh-yue:榻榻米]][[zh:疊蓆]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q318763]] Wikidata name: tatami [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Straw mat used as flooring in Japan}} {{about|the Japanese flooring|the Japanese armour component|Tatami-dō}} [[File:Enkō-ji, Kyoto Inside.jpg|thumb|]]
'''Tatami''' (畳) are types of [[:en:Mat|Mat]] used as flooring material in traditional [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese-style rooms. They are made in standard sizes, twice as long as wide, about 0.9 metres (3') by 1.8 metres (6') depending on the region. In martial arts, tatami are the floor used for training in a [[:en:Dojo|Dojo]] and for competition.<ref>{{Cite web|title=The Quest for the Perfect Judo Floor {{!}} Judo Info|url=https://judoinfo.com/tatami/|website=judoinfo.com|access-date=2020-05-05}}</ref>
Tatami are covered with a [[:en:Weft|Weft]]-faced weave of {{nihongo|[[:en:Juncus effusus|soft rush]]|藺草|igusa}} ([[:en:common rush|common rush]]), on a warp of hemp or weaker cotton. There are four warps per weft [[:en:shed (weaving)|shed]], two at each end (or sometimes two per shed, one at each end, to cut costs). The {{transl|ja|doko}} (core) is traditionally made from sewn-together rice straw, but contemporary tatami sometimes have [[:en:chip board|compressed wood chip boards]] or [[:en:extruded polystyrene foam|extruded polystyrene foam]] in their cores, instead or as well. The long sides are usually {{nihongo|edged|縁|heri}} with [[:en:Brocade|Brocade]] or plain cloth, although some tatami have no edging.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.kyo-tatami.com/english/about|title=Understanding Tatami|date=28 June 2015|access-date=2016-10-31 |publisher=[[Motoyama Tatami shop]] }}</ref><ref name="igusa">{{cite web |title=Structure of Tatami |url=https://kyo-tatami.com/world/about/ |website=kyo-tatami.com |publisher=Motoyama Tatami Shop |access-date=14 June 2021 |date=2015-06-28}}</ref>
<gallery caption=Construction mode=packed> File:Modern tatami.JPG|Machine-sewing of tatami File:Tatami sectional view.jpg|Cross-section of a modern tatami with an [[:en:extruded polystyrene foam|extruded polystyrene foam]] core File:Men Making Tatami Mats, 1860 - ca. 1900.jpg|Making tatami mats, late 19th century. File:Tatami.jpg|Close-up of mat surface and edging </gallery>
==History==
The term ''tatami'' is derived from the verb {{nihongo||畳む|tatamu}}, meaning 'to fold' or 'to pile'. This indicates that the early tatami were thin and could be folded up when not used or piled in layers.<ref name="KoandaTatami">Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan, entry for "tatami".</ref>
Tatami were originally a luxury item for the nobility. The lower classes had mat-covered earthen floors.<ref name="tatamiluxury">{{cite web |url=http://www.yoshinoantiques.com/Interior-article.html |title=The Yoshino Newsletter |access-date=2007-03-28 |work=Floors/Tatami |publisher=[[Yoshino Japanese Antiques]] |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070331133235/http://www.yoshinoantiques.com/Interior-article.html |archive-date=2007-03-31 }}</ref> During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], when the [[:en:Shinden-zukuri|Shinden-zukuri]] architectural style of aristocratic residences was consummated, the flooring of shinden-zukuri palatial rooms were mainly wooden, and tatami were only used as seating for the highest aristocrats.<ref name="Sato Osamu 1994"/>
In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], there arose the [[:en:Shoin-zukuri|Shoin-zukuri]] architectural style of residence for the samurai and priests who had gained power. This architectural style reached its peak of development in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], when tatami gradually came to be spread over whole rooms, beginning with small rooms. Rooms completely spread with tatami came to be known as {{nihongo||座敷||{{lit.|spread out for sitting}}}}, and rules concerning seating and etiquette determined the arrangement of the tatami in the rooms.<ref name="Sato Osamu 1994"/>
It is said that prior to the mid-16th century, the ruling nobility and samurai slept on tatami or woven mats called {{nihongo||茣蓙|goza}}, while commoners used straw mats or loose straw for bedding.<ref>Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan, entry for "bedding"</ref> Tatami were gradually popularized and reached the homes of commoners toward the end of the 17th century.<ref name="tatamicommoners">{{cite web |url=http://www.kcif.or.jp/archive/en/newsletter/lik/archives/0304/04_2003.htm |title=Kyoto International Community House Newsletter |access-date=2007-03-28 |work=2nd section titled History of tatami |publisher=[[Kyoto City International Foundation]] }}</ref>
Houses built in Japan today often have few tatami-floored rooms, if any. Having just one is not uncommon. The rooms having tatami flooring and other such traditional architectural features are referred to as ''nihonma'' or ''[[:en:Washitsu|Washitsu]]'', "Japanese-style rooms".
<gallery mode="packed" heights="200"> File:Saikū Historical Museum - Display item04 - The palace of Saiô - Miniature model.jpg|Green tatami in a museum model of the [[Saikū]] palace in ~the 9th century File:Gaki zoshi - Tokyo - part 1.jpeg|Courtiers making music, [[:wiktionary:circa#English|circa]] 1150-1200 File:Otomo no Yakamochi Agedatamibon.gif|As a dais, ~13th century File:Tengu zoshi, Nezu Museum scroll, detail 2.jpg|An almost-completely-covered floor in an illustration drawn in 1296 File:Sleeping two, Kasuga Gongen Genki (1309).jpg|Tatami being used as sleeping mats, 1309 (see [[:en:Futon|Futon]]) </gallery>
==Size==
The size of tatami traditionally differs between regions in Japan:
* [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]: {{cvt|0.955|by|1.91|m}}, called {{nihongo||京間|Kyōma}} ''tatami'' * [[:en:Nagoya|Nagoya]]: {{cvt|0.91|by|1.82|m}}, called {{nihongo||中京間|Chūkyōma}} or {{nihongo||合の間|Ainoma|{{lit.}} "in-between" size}} ''tatami'' * [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]: {{cvt|0.88|by|1.76|m}}, called {{nihongo||江戸間|Edoma}} or {{nihongo||関東間|Kantōma}} ''tatami''
In terms of thickness, {{cvt|5.5|cm}} is average for a ''Kyōma'' tatami, while {{cvt|6.0|cm}} is the norm for a ''Kantōma'' tatami.<ref name="Sato Osamu 1994">Sato Osamu, "A History of Tatami," in Chanoyu Quarterly no. 77 (1994).</ref> A half mat is called a {{nihongo||半畳|hanjō}}, and a mat of three-quarter length, which is used in tea-ceremony rooms (''[[:en:Chashitsu|Chashitsu]]''), is called ''daimedatami'' ({{lang|ja|大目畳}} or {{lang|ja|台目畳}}).<ref name="KoandaTatami"/> In terms of [[Japanese units of measurement#Length|traditional Japanese length units]], a tatami is (allowing for regional variation) 1 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'' by 0.5 ''ken'', or equivalently 6 ''[[:en:Shaku (unit)|shaku]]'' by 3 ''shaku'' – formally this is {{cvt|1.81818|×|0.90909|m|in|0}}, the size of Nagoya tatami. Note that a ''shaku'' is almost the same length as one foot in the traditional English-American measurement system.
In Japan, the size of a room is often measured by the number of {{nihongo|''tatami'' mats|-畳|-jō}}, about {{cvt|1.653|m2}} for a standard Nagoya-size tatami. Alternatively, in terms of [[Japanese units of measurement#Area|traditional Japanese area units]], room area (and especially house floor area) is measured in terms of ''[[:en:Tsubo|Tsubo]],'' where one ''tsubo'' is the area of two tatami mats (a square); formally 1 ''ken'' by 1 ''ken'' or about {{cvt|3.306|m2}}.
Some common room sizes in the Nagoya region are: *{{frac|4|1|2}} mats = 9 shaku × 9 shaku ≈ {{cvt|2.73|×|2.73|m}} *6 mats = 9 shaku × 12 shaku ≈ {{cvt|2.73|×|3.64|m}} *8 mats = 12 shaku × 12 shaku ≈ {{cvt|3.64|×|3.64|m}}
Shops were traditionally designed to be {{frac|5|1|2}} mats, and [[:en:Chashitsu|tea rooms]] are frequently {{frac|4|1|2}} mats.{{Citation needed|date=July 2009}}
A special format is the [[:en:Ryūkyū|Ryūkyū]] (琉球) ''tatami'', which is in square-size and can have various measurements.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.ryu-kyutatami.com/f/about-ryukyutatami | title=琉球畳とは }}</ref> The Ryūkyū ''tatami'' does not have a border and is therefore becoming popular in modern rooms for its simplicity.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.ikehikojapan.com/blogs/product/l_20210111 | title=Unexpected advantages and disadvantages of Ryukyu tatami mats in traditional traditional Japanese style rooms | date=20 February 2023 }}</ref>
==Layout== There are rules concerning the number of tatami mats and the layout of the tatami mats in a room. {{anchor|auspicious|inauspicious}} In the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], {{nihongo|"auspicious"|祝儀敷き|shūgijiki}} tatami arrangements and {{nihongo|"inauspicious"|不祝儀敷き|fushūgijiki}} tatami arrangements were distinctly differentiated, and the tatami accordingly would be rearranged depending on the occasion. In modern practice, the "auspicious" layout is ordinarily used. In this arrangement, the junctions of the tatami form a "T" shape; in the "inauspicious" arrangement, the tatami are in a grid pattern wherein the junctions form a "+" shape.<ref name="Sato Osamu 1994"/> An auspicious tiling often requires the use of {{frac|1|2}} mats to tile a room.<ref name="tilings">{{cite conference | last1 = Erickson | first1 = Alejandro | last2 = Ruskey | first2 = FRank | author2-link = FRank Ruskey | last3 = Schurch | first3 = Mark | last4 = Woodcock | first4 = Jennifer | editor1-last = Thai | editor1-first = My T. | editor2-last = Sahni | editor2-first = Sartaj | arxiv = 1103.3309 | contribution = Auspicious tatami mat arrangements | doi = 10.1007/978-3-642-14031-0_32 | mr = 2720105 | pages = 288–297 | publisher = Springer | series = Lecture Notes in Computer Science | title = Computing and Combinatorics, 16th Annual International Conference, COCOON 2010, Nha Trang, Vietnam, July 19-21, 2010. Proceedings | volume = 6196 | year = 2010}}</ref> It is [[:en:NP-complete|NP-complete]] to determine whether a large room has an auspicious arrangement using only full mats.<ref>{{cite conference | last1 = Erickson | first1 = Alejandro | last2 = Ruskey | first2 = FRank | author2-link = FRank Ruskey | editor1-last = Lecroq | editor1-first = Thierry | editor2-last = Mouchard | editor2-first = Laurent | arxiv = 1305.6669 | contribution = Domino tatami covering is NP-complete | doi = 10.1007/978-3-642-45278-9_13 | location = Heidelberg | mr = 3162068 | pages = 140–149 | publisher = Springer | series = Lecture Notes in Computer Science | title = Combinatorial Algorithms: 24th International Workshop, IWOCA 2013, Rouen, France, July 10-12, 2013, Revised Selected Papers | volume = 8288 | year = 2013| s2cid = 12738241 }}</ref>
An inauspicious layout was used to avoid bad fortune at inauspicious events, such as funerals. Now it is widely associated with bad luck and itself avoided.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Kalland|first=Arne|date=April 1999|title=Houses, People and Good Fortune: Geomancy and Vernacular Architecture in Japan|journal=Worldviews|volume=3|issue=1|pages=33–50|jstor=43809122|doi=10.1163/156853599X00036}}</ref> <gallery mode=packed heights=200> File:JapanHomes101 ARRANGEMENT OF MATS IN DIFFERENT-SIZED ROOMS.jpg|upright=0.3|Some auspicious layouts from the early 1800s ([[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]) Image:Tatami layout 1.svg|One possible auspicious layout of a {{frac|4|1|2}} mat room {{legend|#ff7f7f|Half mat}}{{legend|#4992ff|Full mat}} Image:Tearoom layout.svg|Typical layout of a {{frac|4|1|2}} mat tea room in the cold season, when the hearth built into the floor is in use. The room has a ''[[tokonoma]]'' and ''[[Mizuya#The special mizuya dōko|mizuya dōko]]'' Image:Youkoukan06n4592.jpg|Room with tatami flooring in an inauspicious layout and paper doors ([[:en:Shōji|Shōji]]) File:Anforet 3F 2017-06-02 ac (2).jpg|An auspicious layout File:ITatami (8465411258).jpg|"T" shape File:Tatami (51687126569).jpg|Ryūkyū ''tatami'' are square shaped without borders </gallery>

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q318763]] Wikidata name: tatami [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Straw mat used as flooring in Japan}} {{about|the Japanese flooring|the Japanese armour component|Tatami-dō}} [[File:Enkō-ji, Kyoto Inside.jpg|thumb|]]
'''Tatami''' (畳) are types of [[:en:Mat|Mat]] used as flooring material in traditional [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese-style rooms. They are made in standard sizes, twice as long as wide, about 0.9 metres (3') by 1.8 metres (6') depending on the region. In martial arts, tatami are the floor used for training in a [[:en:Dojo|Dojo]] and for competition.<ref>{{Cite web|title=The Quest for the Perfect Judo Floor {{!}} Judo Info|url=https://judoinfo.com/tatami/|website=judoinfo.com|access-date=2020-05-05}}</ref>
Tatami are covered with a [[:en:Weft|Weft]]-faced weave of {{nihongo|[[:en:Juncus effusus|soft rush]]|藺草|igusa}} ([[:en:common rush|common rush]]), on a warp of hemp or weaker cotton. There are four warps per weft [[:en:shed (weaving)|shed]], two at each end (or sometimes two per shed, one at each end, to cut costs). The {{transl|ja|doko}} (core) is traditionally made from sewn-together rice straw, but contemporary tatami sometimes have [[:en:chip board|compressed wood chip boards]] or [[:en:extruded polystyrene foam|extruded polystyrene foam]] in their cores, instead or as well. The long sides are usually {{nihongo|edged|縁|heri}} with [[:en:Brocade|Brocade]] or plain cloth, although some tatami have no edging.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.kyo-tatami.com/english/about|title=Understanding Tatami|date=28 June 2015|access-date=2016-10-31 |publisher=[[Motoyama Tatami shop]] }}</ref><ref name="igusa">{{cite web |title=Structure of Tatami |url=https://kyo-tatami.com/world/about/ |website=kyo-tatami.com |publisher=Motoyama Tatami Shop |access-date=14 June 2021 |date=2015-06-28}}</ref>
<gallery caption=Construction mode=packed> File:Modern tatami.JPG|Machine-sewing of tatami File:Tatami sectional view.jpg|Cross-section of a modern tatami with an [[:en:extruded polystyrene foam|extruded polystyrene foam]] core File:Men Making Tatami Mats, 1860 - ca. 1900.jpg|Making tatami mats, late 19th century. File:Tatami.jpg|Close-up of mat surface and edging </gallery>
==History==
The term ''tatami'' is derived from the verb {{nihongo||畳む|tatamu}}, meaning 'to fold' or 'to pile'. This indicates that the early tatami were thin and could be folded up when not used or piled in layers.<ref name="KoandaTatami">Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan, entry for "tatami".</ref>
Tatami were originally a luxury item for the nobility. The lower classes had mat-covered earthen floors.<ref name="tatamiluxury">{{cite web |url=http://www.yoshinoantiques.com/Interior-article.html |title=The Yoshino Newsletter |access-date=2007-03-28 |work=Floors/Tatami |publisher=[[Yoshino Japanese Antiques]] |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070331133235/http://www.yoshinoantiques.com/Interior-article.html |archive-date=2007-03-31 }}</ref> During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], when the [[:en:Shinden-zukuri|Shinden-zukuri]] architectural style of aristocratic residences was consummated, the flooring of shinden-zukuri palatial rooms were mainly wooden, and tatami were only used as seating for the highest aristocrats.<ref name="Sato Osamu 1994"/>
In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], there arose the [[:en:Shoin-zukuri|Shoin-zukuri]] architectural style of residence for the samurai and priests who had gained power. This architectural style reached its peak of development in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], when tatami gradually came to be spread over whole rooms, beginning with small rooms. Rooms completely spread with tatami came to be known as {{nihongo||座敷||{{lit.|spread out for sitting}}}}, and rules concerning seating and etiquette determined the arrangement of the tatami in the rooms.<ref name="Sato Osamu 1994"/>
It is said that prior to the mid-16th century, the ruling nobility and samurai slept on tatami or woven mats called {{nihongo||茣蓙|goza}}, while commoners used straw mats or loose straw for bedding.<ref>Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan, entry for "bedding"</ref> Tatami were gradually popularized and reached the homes of commoners toward the end of the 17th century.<ref name="tatamicommoners">{{cite web |url=http://www.kcif.or.jp/archive/en/newsletter/lik/archives/0304/04_2003.htm |title=Kyoto International Community House Newsletter |access-date=2007-03-28 |work=2nd section titled History of tatami |publisher=[[Kyoto City International Foundation]] }}</ref>
Houses built in Japan today often have few tatami-floored rooms, if any. Having just one is not uncommon. The rooms having tatami flooring and other such traditional architectural features are referred to as ''nihonma'' or ''[[:en:Washitsu|Washitsu]]'', "Japanese-style rooms".
<gallery mode="packed" heights="200"> File:Saikū Historical Museum - Display item04 - The palace of Saiô - Miniature model.jpg|Green tatami in a museum model of the [[Saikū]] palace in ~the 9th century File:Gaki zoshi - Tokyo - part 1.jpeg|Courtiers making music, [[:wiktionary:circa#English|circa]] 1150-1200 File:Otomo no Yakamochi Agedatamibon.gif|As a dais, ~13th century File:Tengu zoshi, Nezu Museum scroll, detail 2.jpg|An almost-completely-covered floor in an illustration drawn in 1296 File:Sleeping two, Kasuga Gongen Genki (1309).jpg|Tatami being used as sleeping mats, 1309 (see [[:en:Futon|Futon]]) </gallery>
==Size==
The size of tatami traditionally differs between regions in Japan:
* [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]: {{cvt|0.955|by|1.91|m}}, called {{nihongo||京間|Kyōma}} ''tatami'' * [[:en:Nagoya|Nagoya]]: {{cvt|0.91|by|1.82|m}}, called {{nihongo||中京間|Chūkyōma}} or {{nihongo||合の間|Ainoma|{{lit.}} "in-between" size}} ''tatami'' * [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]: {{cvt|0.88|by|1.76|m}}, called {{nihongo||江戸間|Edoma}} or {{nihongo||関東間|Kantōma}} ''tatami''
In terms of thickness, {{cvt|5.5|cm}} is average for a ''Kyōma'' tatami, while {{cvt|6.0|cm}} is the norm for a ''Kantōma'' tatami.<ref name="Sato Osamu 1994">Sato Osamu, "A History of Tatami," in Chanoyu Quarterly no. 77 (1994).</ref> A half mat is called a {{nihongo||半畳|hanjō}}, and a mat of three-quarter length, which is used in tea-ceremony rooms (''[[:en:Chashitsu|Chashitsu]]''), is called ''daimedatami'' ({{lang|ja|大目畳}} or {{lang|ja|台目畳}}).<ref name="KoandaTatami"/> In terms of [[Japanese units of measurement#Length|traditional Japanese length units]], a tatami is (allowing for regional variation) 1 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'' by 0.5 ''ken'', or equivalently 6 ''[[:en:Shaku (unit)|shaku]]'' by 3 ''shaku'' – formally this is {{cvt|1.81818|×|0.90909|m|in|0}}, the size of Nagoya tatami. Note that a ''shaku'' is almost the same length as one foot in the traditional English-American measurement system.
In Japan, the size of a room is often measured by the number of {{nihongo|''tatami'' mats|-畳|-jō}}, about {{cvt|1.653|m2}} for a standard Nagoya-size tatami. Alternatively, in terms of [[Japanese units of measurement#Area|traditional Japanese area units]], room area (and especially house floor area) is measured in terms of ''[[:en:Tsubo|Tsubo]],'' where one ''tsubo'' is the area of two tatami mats (a square); formally 1 ''ken'' by 1 ''ken'' or about {{cvt|3.306|m2}}.
Some common room sizes in the Nagoya region are: *{{frac|4|1|2}} mats = 9 shaku × 9 shaku ≈ {{cvt|2.73|×|2.73|m}} *6 mats = 9 shaku × 12 shaku ≈ {{cvt|2.73|×|3.64|m}} *8 mats = 12 shaku × 12 shaku ≈ {{cvt|3.64|×|3.64|m}}
Shops were traditionally designed to be {{frac|5|1|2}} mats, and [[:en:Chashitsu|tea rooms]] are frequently {{frac|4|1|2}} mats.{{Citation needed|date=July 2009}}
A special format is the [[:en:Ryūkyū|Ryūkyū]] (琉球) ''tatami'', which is in square-size and can have various measurements.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.ryu-kyutatami.com/f/about-ryukyutatami | title=琉球畳とは }}</ref> The Ryūkyū ''tatami'' does not have a border and is therefore becoming popular in modern rooms for its simplicity.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.ikehikojapan.com/blogs/product/l_20210111 | title=Unexpected advantages and disadvantages of Ryukyu tatami mats in traditional traditional Japanese style rooms | date=20 February 2023 }}</ref>
==Layout== There are rules concerning the number of tatami mats and the layout of the tatami mats in a room. {{anchor|auspicious|inauspicious}} In the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], {{nihongo|"auspicious"|祝儀敷き|shūgijiki}} tatami arrangements and {{nihongo|"inauspicious"|不祝儀敷き|fushūgijiki}} tatami arrangements were distinctly differentiated, and the tatami accordingly would be rearranged depending on the occasion. In modern practice, the "auspicious" layout is ordinarily used. In this arrangement, the junctions of the tatami form a "T" shape; in the "inauspicious" arrangement, the tatami are in a grid pattern wherein the junctions form a "+" shape.<ref name="Sato Osamu 1994"/> An auspicious tiling often requires the use of {{frac|1|2}} mats to tile a room.<ref name="tilings">{{cite conference | last1 = Erickson | first1 = Alejandro | last2 = Ruskey | first2 = FRank | author2-link = FRank Ruskey | last3 = Schurch | first3 = Mark | last4 = Woodcock | first4 = Jennifer | editor1-last = Thai | editor1-first = My T. | editor2-last = Sahni | editor2-first = Sartaj | arxiv = 1103.3309 | contribution = Auspicious tatami mat arrangements | doi = 10.1007/978-3-642-14031-0_32 | mr = 2720105 | pages = 288–297 | publisher = Springer | series = Lecture Notes in Computer Science | title = Computing and Combinatorics, 16th Annual International Conference, COCOON 2010, Nha Trang, Vietnam, July 19-21, 2010. Proceedings | volume = 6196 | year = 2010}}</ref> It is [[:en:NP-complete|NP-complete]] to determine whether a large room has an auspicious arrangement using only full mats.<ref>{{cite conference | last1 = Erickson | first1 = Alejandro | last2 = Ruskey | first2 = FRank | author2-link = FRank Ruskey | editor1-last = Lecroq | editor1-first = Thierry | editor2-last = Mouchard | editor2-first = Laurent | arxiv = 1305.6669 | contribution = Domino tatami covering is NP-complete | doi = 10.1007/978-3-642-45278-9_13 | location = Heidelberg | mr = 3162068 | pages = 140–149 | publisher = Springer | series = Lecture Notes in Computer Science | title = Combinatorial Algorithms: 24th International Workshop, IWOCA 2013, Rouen, France, July 10-12, 2013, Revised Selected Papers | volume = 8288 | year = 2013| s2cid = 12738241 }}</ref>
An inauspicious layout was used to avoid bad fortune at inauspicious events, such as funerals. Now it is widely associated with bad luck and itself avoided.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Kalland|first=Arne|date=April 1999|title=Houses, People and Good Fortune: Geomancy and Vernacular Architecture in Japan|journal=Worldviews|volume=3|issue=1|pages=33–50|jstor=43809122|doi=10.1163/156853599X00036}}</ref> <gallery mode=packed heights=200> File:JapanHomes101 ARRANGEMENT OF MATS IN DIFFERENT-SIZED ROOMS.jpg|upright=0.3|Some auspicious layouts from the early 1800s ([[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]) Image:Tatami layout 1.svg|One possible auspicious layout of a {{frac|4|1|2}} mat room {{legend|#ff7f7f|Half mat}}{{legend|#4992ff|Full mat}} Image:Tearoom layout.svg|Typical layout of a {{frac|4|1|2}} mat tea room in the cold season, when the hearth built into the floor is in use. The room has a ''[[tokonoma]]'' and ''[[Mizuya#The special mizuya dōko|mizuya dōko]]'' Image:Youkoukan06n4592.jpg|Room with tatami flooring in an inauspicious layout and paper doors ([[:en:Shōji|Shōji]]) File:Anforet 3F 2017-06-02 ac (2).jpg|An auspicious layout File:ITatami (8465411258).jpg|"T" shape File:Tatami (51687126569).jpg|Ryūkyū ''tatami'' are square shaped without borders </gallery>

'''Tatami''' (畳) are types of [[:en:Mat|Mat]] used as flooring material in traditional [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese-style rooms. They are made in standard sizes, twice as long as wide, about 0.9 metres (3') by 1.8 metres (6') depending on the region. In martial arts, tatami are the floor used for training in a [[:en:Dojo|Dojo]] and for competition.<ref>{{Cite web|title=The Quest for the Perfect Judo Floor {{!}} Judo Info|url=https://judoinfo.com/tatami/|website=judoinfo.com|access-date=2020-05-05}}</ref>
Tatami are covered with a [[:en:Weft|Weft]]-faced weave of {{nihongo|[[:en:Juncus effusus|soft rush]]|藺草|igusa}} ([[:en:common rush|common rush]]), on a warp of hemp or weaker cotton. There are four warps per weft [[:en:shed (weaving)|shed]], two at each end (or sometimes two per shed, one at each end, to cut costs). The {{transl|ja|doko}} (core) is traditionally made from sewn-together rice straw, but contemporary tatami sometimes have [[:en:chip board|compressed wood chip boards]] or [[:en:extruded polystyrene foam|extruded polystyrene foam]] in their cores, instead or as well. The long sides are usually {{nihongo|edged|縁|heri}} with [[:en:Brocade|Brocade]] or plain cloth, although some tatami have no edging.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.kyo-tatami.com/english/about|title=Understanding Tatami|date=28 June 2015|access-date=2016-10-31 |publisher=[[Motoyama Tatami shop]] }}</ref><ref name="igusa">{{cite web |title=Structure of Tatami |url=https://kyo-tatami.com/world/about/ |website=kyo-tatami.com |publisher=Motoyama Tatami Shop |access-date=14 June 2021 |date=2015-06-28}}</ref>

==History==
The term ''tatami'' is derived from the verb {{nihongo||畳む|tatamu}}, meaning 'to fold' or 'to pile'. This indicates that the early tatami were thin and could be folded up when not used or piled in layers.<ref name="KoandaTatami">Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan, entry for "tatami".</ref>
Tatami were originally a luxury item for the nobility. The lower classes had mat-covered earthen floors.<ref name="tatamiluxury">{{cite web |url=http://www.yoshinoantiques.com/Interior-article.html |title=The Yoshino Newsletter |access-date=2007-03-28 |work=Floors/Tatami |publisher=[[Yoshino Japanese Antiques]] |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070331133235/http://www.yoshinoantiques.com/Interior-article.html |archive-date=2007-03-31 }}</ref> During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], when the [[:en:Shinden-zukuri|Shinden-zukuri]] architectural style of aristocratic residences was consummated, the flooring of shinden-zukuri palatial rooms were mainly wooden, and tatami were only used as seating for the highest aristocrats.<ref name="Sato Osamu 1994"/>
In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], there arose the [[:en:Shoin-zukuri|Shoin-zukuri]] architectural style of residence for the samurai and priests who had gained power. This architectural style reached its peak of development in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], when tatami gradually came to be spread over whole rooms, beginning with small rooms. Rooms completely spread with tatami came to be known as {{nihongo||座敷||{{lit.|spread out for sitting}}}}, and rules concerning seating and etiquette determined the arrangement of the tatami in the rooms.<ref name="Sato Osamu 1994"/>
It is said that prior to the mid-16th century, the ruling nobility and samurai slept on tatami or woven mats called {{nihongo||茣蓙|goza}}, while commoners used straw mats or loose straw for bedding.<ref>Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan, entry for "bedding"</ref> Tatami were gradually popularized and reached the homes of commoners toward the end of the 17th century.<ref name="tatamicommoners">{{cite web |url=http://www.kcif.or.jp/archive/en/newsletter/lik/archives/0304/04_2003.htm |title=Kyoto International Community House Newsletter |access-date=2007-03-28 |work=2nd section titled History of tatami |publisher=[[Kyoto City International Foundation]] }}</ref>
Houses built in Japan today often have few tatami-floored rooms, if any. Having just one is not uncommon. The rooms having tatami flooring and other such traditional architectural features are referred to as ''nihonma'' or ''[[:en:Washitsu|Washitsu]]'', "Japanese-style rooms".
<gallery mode="packed" heights="200"> File:Saikū Historical Museum - Display item04 - The palace of Saiô - Miniature model.jpg|Green tatami in a museum model of the [[Saikū]] palace in ~the 9th century File:Gaki zoshi - Tokyo - part 1.jpeg|Courtiers making music, [[:wiktionary:circa#English|circa]] 1150-1200 File:Otomo no Yakamochi Agedatamibon.gif|As a dais, ~13th century File:Tengu zoshi, Nezu Museum scroll, detail 2.jpg|An almost-completely-covered floor in an illustration drawn in 1296 File:Sleeping two, Kasuga Gongen Genki (1309).jpg|Tatami being used as sleeping mats, 1309 (see [[:en:Futon|Futon]]) </gallery>
==Size==
The size of tatami traditionally differs between regions in Japan:
* [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]: {{cvt|0.955|by|1.91|m}}, called {{nihongo||京間|Kyōma}} ''tatami'' * [[:en:Nagoya|Nagoya]]: {{cvt|0.91|by|1.82|m}}, called {{nihongo||中京間|Chūkyōma}} or {{nihongo||合の間|Ainoma|{{lit.}} "in-between" size}} ''tatami'' * [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]: {{cvt|0.88|by|1.76|m}}, called {{nihongo||江戸間|Edoma}} or {{nihongo||関東間|Kantōma}} ''tatami''
In terms of thickness, {{cvt|5.5|cm}} is average for a ''Kyōma'' tatami, while {{cvt|6.0|cm}} is the norm for a ''Kantōma'' tatami.<ref name="Sato Osamu 1994">Sato Osamu, "A History of Tatami," in Chanoyu Quarterly no. 77 (1994).</ref> A half mat is called a {{nihongo||半畳|hanjō}}, and a mat of three-quarter length, which is used in tea-ceremony rooms (''[[:en:Chashitsu|Chashitsu]]''), is called ''daimedatami'' ({{lang|ja|大目畳}} or {{lang|ja|台目畳}}).<ref name="KoandaTatami"/> In terms of [[Japanese units of measurement#Length|traditional Japanese length units]], a tatami is (allowing for regional variation) 1 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'' by 0.5 ''ken'', or equivalently 6 ''[[:en:Shaku (unit)|shaku]]'' by 3 ''shaku'' – formally this is {{cvt|1.81818|×|0.90909|m|in|0}}, the size of Nagoya tatami. Note that a ''shaku'' is almost the same length as one foot in the traditional English-American measurement system.
In Japan, the size of a room is often measured by the number of {{nihongo|''tatami'' mats|-畳|-jō}}, about {{cvt|1.653|m2}} for a standard Nagoya-size tatami. Alternatively, in terms of [[Japanese units of measurement#Area|traditional Japanese area units]], room area (and especially house floor area) is measured in terms of ''[[:en:Tsubo|Tsubo]],'' where one ''tsubo'' is the area of two tatami mats (a square); formally 1 ''ken'' by 1 ''ken'' or about {{cvt|3.306|m2}}.
Some common room sizes in the Nagoya region are: *{{frac|4|1|2}} mats = 9 shaku × 9 shaku ≈ {{cvt|2.73|×|2.73|m}} *6 mats = 9 shaku × 12 shaku ≈ {{cvt|2.73|×|3.64|m}} *8 mats = 12 shaku × 12 shaku ≈ {{cvt|3.64|×|3.64|m}}
Shops were traditionally designed to be {{frac|5|1|2}} mats, and [[:en:Chashitsu|tea rooms]] are frequently {{frac|4|1|2}} mats.{{Citation needed|date=July 2009}}
A special format is the [[:en:Ryūkyū|Ryūkyū]] (琉球) ''tatami'', which is in square-size and can have various measurements.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.ryu-kyutatami.com/f/about-ryukyutatami | title=琉球畳とは }}</ref> The Ryūkyū ''tatami'' does not have a border and is therefore becoming popular in modern rooms for its simplicity.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.ikehikojapan.com/blogs/product/l_20210111 | title=Unexpected advantages and disadvantages of Ryukyu tatami mats in traditional traditional Japanese style rooms | date=20 February 2023 }}</ref>
==Layout== There are rules concerning the number of tatami mats and the layout of the tatami mats in a room. {{anchor|auspicious|inauspicious}} In the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], {{nihongo|"auspicious"|祝儀敷き|shūgijiki}} tatami arrangements and {{nihongo|"inauspicious"|不祝儀敷き|fushūgijiki}} tatami arrangements were distinctly differentiated, and the tatami accordingly would be rearranged depending on the occasion. In modern practice, the "auspicious" layout is ordinarily used. In this arrangement, the junctions of the tatami form a "T" shape; in the "inauspicious" arrangement, the tatami are in a grid pattern wherein the junctions form a "+" shape.<ref name="Sato Osamu 1994"/> An auspicious tiling often requires the use of {{frac|1|2}} mats to tile a room.<ref name="tilings">{{cite conference | last1 = Erickson | first1 = Alejandro | last2 = Ruskey | first2 = FRank | author2-link = FRank Ruskey | last3 = Schurch | first3 = Mark | last4 = Woodcock | first4 = Jennifer | editor1-last = Thai | editor1-first = My T. | editor2-last = Sahni | editor2-first = Sartaj | arxiv = 1103.3309 | contribution = Auspicious tatami mat arrangements | doi = 10.1007/978-3-642-14031-0_32 | mr = 2720105 | pages = 288–297 | publisher = Springer | series = Lecture Notes in Computer Science | title = Computing and Combinatorics, 16th Annual International Conference, COCOON 2010, Nha Trang, Vietnam, July 19-21, 2010. Proceedings | volume = 6196 | year = 2010}}</ref> It is [[:en:NP-complete|NP-complete]] to determine whether a large room has an auspicious arrangement using only full mats.<ref>{{cite conference | last1 = Erickson | first1 = Alejandro | last2 = Ruskey | first2 = FRank | author2-link = FRank Ruskey | editor1-last = Lecroq | editor1-first = Thierry | editor2-last = Mouchard | editor2-first = Laurent | arxiv = 1305.6669 | contribution = Domino tatami covering is NP-complete | doi = 10.1007/978-3-642-45278-9_13 | location = Heidelberg | mr = 3162068 | pages = 140–149 | publisher = Springer | series = Lecture Notes in Computer Science | title = Combinatorial Algorithms: 24th International Workshop, IWOCA 2013, Rouen, France, July 10-12, 2013, Revised Selected Papers | volume = 8288 | year = 2013| s2cid = 12738241 }}</ref>
An inauspicious layout was used to avoid bad fortune at inauspicious events, such as funerals. Now it is widely associated with bad luck and itself avoided.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Kalland|first=Arne|date=April 1999|title=Houses, People and Good Fortune: Geomancy and Vernacular Architecture in Japan|journal=Worldviews|volume=3|issue=1|pages=33–50|jstor=43809122|doi=10.1163/156853599X00036}}</ref> <gallery mode=packed heights=200> File:JapanHomes101 ARRANGEMENT OF MATS IN DIFFERENT-SIZED ROOMS.jpg|upright=0.3|Some auspicious layouts from the early 1800s ([[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]) Image:Tatami layout 1.svg|One possible auspicious layout of a {{frac|4|1|2}} mat room {{legend|#ff7f7f|Half mat}}{{legend|#4992ff|Full mat}} Image:Tearoom layout.svg|Typical layout of a {{frac|4|1|2}} mat tea room in the cold season, when the hearth built into the floor is in use. The room has a ''[[tokonoma]]'' and ''[[Mizuya#The special mizuya dōko|mizuya dōko]]'' Image:Youkoukan06n4592.jpg|Room with tatami flooring in an inauspicious layout and paper doors ([[:en:Shōji|Shōji]]) File:Anforet 3F 2017-06-02 ac (2).jpg|An auspicious layout File:ITatami (8465411258).jpg|"T" shape File:Tatami (51687126569).jpg|Ryūkyū ''tatami'' are square shaped without borders </gallery>
==See also== * [[:en:Higashiyama Bunka|Higashiyama Bunka]] in [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q318763]] Wikidata name: tatami [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Straw mat used as flooring in Japan}} {{about|the Japanese flooring|the Japanese armour component|Tatami-dō}} [[File:Enkō-ji, Kyoto Inside.jpg|thumb|]]
'''Tatami''' (畳) are types of [[:en:Mat|Mat]] used as flooring material in traditional [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese-style rooms. They are made in standard sizes, twice as long as wide, about 0.9 metres (3') by 1.8 metres (6') depending on the region. In martial arts, tatami are the floor used for training in a [[:en:Dojo|Dojo]] and for competition.<ref>{{Cite web|title=The Quest for the Perfect Judo Floor {{!}} Judo Info|url=https://judoinfo.com/tatami/|website=judoinfo.com|access-date=2020-05-05}}</ref>
Tatami are covered with a [[:en:Weft|Weft]]-faced weave of {{nihongo|[[:en:Juncus effusus|soft rush]]|藺草|igusa}} ([[:en:common rush|common rush]]), on a warp of hemp or weaker cotton. There are four warps per weft [[:en:shed (weaving)|shed]], two at each end (or sometimes two per shed, one at each end, to cut costs). The {{transl|ja|doko}} (core) is traditionally made from sewn-together rice straw, but contemporary tatami sometimes have [[:en:chip board|compressed wood chip boards]] or [[:en:extruded polystyrene foam|extruded polystyrene foam]] in their cores, instead or as well. The long sides are usually {{nihongo|edged|縁|heri}} with [[:en:Brocade|Brocade]] or plain cloth, although some tatami have no edging.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.kyo-tatami.com/english/about|title=Understanding Tatami|date=28 June 2015|access-date=2016-10-31 |publisher=[[Motoyama Tatami shop]] }}</ref><ref name="igusa">{{cite web |title=Structure of Tatami |url=https://kyo-tatami.com/world/about/ |website=kyo-tatami.com |publisher=Motoyama Tatami Shop |access-date=14 June 2021 |date=2015-06-28}}</ref>
<gallery caption=Construction mode=packed> File:Modern tatami.JPG|Machine-sewing of tatami File:Tatami sectional view.jpg|Cross-section of a modern tatami with an [[:en:extruded polystyrene foam|extruded polystyrene foam]] core File:Men Making Tatami Mats, 1860 - ca. 1900.jpg|Making tatami mats, late 19th century. File:Tatami.jpg|Close-up of mat surface and edging </gallery>
==History==
The term ''tatami'' is derived from the verb {{nihongo||畳む|tatamu}}, meaning 'to fold' or 'to pile'. This indicates that the early tatami were thin and could be folded up when not used or piled in layers.<ref name="KoandaTatami">Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan, entry for "tatami".</ref>
Tatami were originally a luxury item for the nobility. The lower classes had mat-covered earthen floors.<ref name="tatamiluxury">{{cite web |url=http://www.yoshinoantiques.com/Interior-article.html |title=The Yoshino Newsletter |access-date=2007-03-28 |work=Floors/Tatami |publisher=[[Yoshino Japanese Antiques]] |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070331133235/http://www.yoshinoantiques.com/Interior-article.html |archive-date=2007-03-31 }}</ref> During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], when the [[:en:Shinden-zukuri|Shinden-zukuri]] architectural style of aristocratic residences was consummated, the flooring of shinden-zukuri palatial rooms were mainly wooden, and tatami were only used as seating for the highest aristocrats.<ref name="Sato Osamu 1994"/>
In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], there arose the [[:en:Shoin-zukuri|Shoin-zukuri]] architectural style of residence for the samurai and priests who had gained power. This architectural style reached its peak of development in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], when tatami gradually came to be spread over whole rooms, beginning with small rooms. Rooms completely spread with tatami came to be known as {{nihongo||座敷||{{lit.|spread out for sitting}}}}, and rules concerning seating and etiquette determined the arrangement of the tatami in the rooms.<ref name="Sato Osamu 1994"/>
It is said that prior to the mid-16th century, the ruling nobility and samurai slept on tatami or woven mats called {{nihongo||茣蓙|goza}}, while commoners used straw mats or loose straw for bedding.<ref>Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan, entry for "bedding"</ref> Tatami were gradually popularized and reached the homes of commoners toward the end of the 17th century.<ref name="tatamicommoners">{{cite web |url=http://www.kcif.or.jp/archive/en/newsletter/lik/archives/0304/04_2003.htm |title=Kyoto International Community House Newsletter |access-date=2007-03-28 |work=2nd section titled History of tatami |publisher=[[Kyoto City International Foundation]] }}</ref>
Houses built in Japan today often have few tatami-floored rooms, if any. Having just one is not uncommon. The rooms having tatami flooring and other such traditional architectural features are referred to as ''nihonma'' or ''[[:en:Washitsu|Washitsu]]'', "Japanese-style rooms".
<gallery mode="packed" heights="200"> File:Saikū Historical Museum - Display item04 - The palace of Saiô - Miniature model.jpg|Green tatami in a museum model of the [[Saikū]] palace in ~the 9th century File:Gaki zoshi - Tokyo - part 1.jpeg|Courtiers making music, [[:wiktionary:circa#English|circa]] 1150-1200 File:Otomo no Yakamochi Agedatamibon.gif|As a dais, ~13th century File:Tengu zoshi, Nezu Museum scroll, detail 2.jpg|An almost-completely-covered floor in an illustration drawn in 1296 File:Sleeping two, Kasuga Gongen Genki (1309).jpg|Tatami being used as sleeping mats, 1309 (see [[:en:Futon|Futon]]) </gallery>
==Size==
The size of tatami traditionally differs between regions in Japan:
* [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]: {{cvt|0.955|by|1.91|m}}, called {{nihongo||京間|Kyōma}} ''tatami'' * [[:en:Nagoya|Nagoya]]: {{cvt|0.91|by|1.82|m}}, called {{nihongo||中京間|Chūkyōma}} or {{nihongo||合の間|Ainoma|{{lit.}} "in-between" size}} ''tatami'' * [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]: {{cvt|0.88|by|1.76|m}}, called {{nihongo||江戸間|Edoma}} or {{nihongo||関東間|Kantōma}} ''tatami''
In terms of thickness, {{cvt|5.5|cm}} is average for a ''Kyōma'' tatami, while {{cvt|6.0|cm}} is the norm for a ''Kantōma'' tatami.<ref name="Sato Osamu 1994">Sato Osamu, "A History of Tatami," in Chanoyu Quarterly no. 77 (1994).</ref> A half mat is called a {{nihongo||半畳|hanjō}}, and a mat of three-quarter length, which is used in tea-ceremony rooms (''[[:en:Chashitsu|Chashitsu]]''), is called ''daimedatami'' ({{lang|ja|大目畳}} or {{lang|ja|台目畳}}).<ref name="KoandaTatami"/> In terms of [[Japanese units of measurement#Length|traditional Japanese length units]], a tatami is (allowing for regional variation) 1 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'' by 0.5 ''ken'', or equivalently 6 ''[[:en:Shaku (unit)|shaku]]'' by 3 ''shaku'' – formally this is {{cvt|1.81818|×|0.90909|m|in|0}}, the size of Nagoya tatami. Note that a ''shaku'' is almost the same length as one foot in the traditional English-American measurement system.
In Japan, the size of a room is often measured by the number of {{nihongo|''tatami'' mats|-畳|-jō}}, about {{cvt|1.653|m2}} for a standard Nagoya-size tatami. Alternatively, in terms of [[Japanese units of measurement#Area|traditional Japanese area units]], room area (and especially house floor area) is measured in terms of ''[[:en:Tsubo|Tsubo]],'' where one ''tsubo'' is the area of two tatami mats (a square); formally 1 ''ken'' by 1 ''ken'' or about {{cvt|3.306|m2}}.
Some common room sizes in the Nagoya region are: *{{frac|4|1|2}} mats = 9 shaku × 9 shaku ≈ {{cvt|2.73|×|2.73|m}} *6 mats = 9 shaku × 12 shaku ≈ {{cvt|2.73|×|3.64|m}} *8 mats = 12 shaku × 12 shaku ≈ {{cvt|3.64|×|3.64|m}}
Shops were traditionally designed to be {{frac|5|1|2}} mats, and [[:en:Chashitsu|tea rooms]] are frequently {{frac|4|1|2}} mats.{{Citation needed|date=July 2009}}
A special format is the [[:en:Ryūkyū|Ryūkyū]] (琉球) ''tatami'', which is in square-size and can have various measurements.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.ryu-kyutatami.com/f/about-ryukyutatami | title=琉球畳とは }}</ref> The Ryūkyū ''tatami'' does not have a border and is therefore becoming popular in modern rooms for its simplicity.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.ikehikojapan.com/blogs/product/l_20210111 | title=Unexpected advantages and disadvantages of Ryukyu tatami mats in traditional traditional Japanese style rooms | date=20 February 2023 }}</ref>
==Layout== There are rules concerning the number of tatami mats and the layout of the tatami mats in a room. {{anchor|auspicious|inauspicious}} In the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], {{nihongo|"auspicious"|祝儀敷き|shūgijiki}} tatami arrangements and {{nihongo|"inauspicious"|不祝儀敷き|fushūgijiki}} tatami arrangements were distinctly differentiated, and the tatami accordingly would be rearranged depending on the occasion. In modern practice, the "auspicious" layout is ordinarily used. In this arrangement, the junctions of the tatami form a "T" shape; in the "inauspicious" arrangement, the tatami are in a grid pattern wherein the junctions form a "+" shape.<ref name="Sato Osamu 1994"/> An auspicious tiling often requires the use of {{frac|1|2}} mats to tile a room.<ref name="tilings">{{cite conference | last1 = Erickson | first1 = Alejandro | last2 = Ruskey | first2 = FRank | author2-link = FRank Ruskey | last3 = Schurch | first3 = Mark | last4 = Woodcock | first4 = Jennifer | editor1-last = Thai | editor1-first = My T. | editor2-last = Sahni | editor2-first = Sartaj | arxiv = 1103.3309 | contribution = Auspicious tatami mat arrangements | doi = 10.1007/978-3-642-14031-0_32 | mr = 2720105 | pages = 288–297 | publisher = Springer | series = Lecture Notes in Computer Science | title = Computing and Combinatorics, 16th Annual International Conference, COCOON 2010, Nha Trang, Vietnam, July 19-21, 2010. Proceedings | volume = 6196 | year = 2010}}</ref> It is [[:en:NP-complete|NP-complete]] to determine whether a large room has an auspicious arrangement using only full mats.<ref>{{cite conference | last1 = Erickson | first1 = Alejandro | last2 = Ruskey | first2 = FRank | author2-link = FRank Ruskey | editor1-last = Lecroq | editor1-first = Thierry | editor2-last = Mouchard | editor2-first = Laurent | arxiv = 1305.6669 | contribution = Domino tatami covering is NP-complete | doi = 10.1007/978-3-642-45278-9_13 | location = Heidelberg | mr = 3162068 | pages = 140–149 | publisher = Springer | series = Lecture Notes in Computer Science | title = Combinatorial Algorithms: 24th International Workshop, IWOCA 2013, Rouen, France, July 10-12, 2013, Revised Selected Papers | volume = 8288 | year = 2013| s2cid = 12738241 }}</ref>
An inauspicious layout was used to avoid bad fortune at inauspicious events, such as funerals. Now it is widely associated with bad luck and itself avoided.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Kalland|first=Arne|date=April 1999|title=Houses, People and Good Fortune: Geomancy and Vernacular Architecture in Japan|journal=Worldviews|volume=3|issue=1|pages=33–50|jstor=43809122|doi=10.1163/156853599X00036}}</ref> <gallery mode=packed heights=200> File:JapanHomes101 ARRANGEMENT OF MATS IN DIFFERENT-SIZED ROOMS.jpg|upright=0.3|Some auspicious layouts from the early 1800s ([[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]) Image:Tatami layout 1.svg|One possible auspicious layout of a {{frac|4|1|2}} mat room {{legend|#ff7f7f|Half mat}}{{legend|#4992ff|Full mat}} Image:Tearoom layout.svg|Typical layout of a {{frac|4|1|2}} mat tea room in the cold season, when the hearth built into the floor is in use. The room has a ''[[tokonoma]]'' and ''[[Mizuya#The special mizuya dōko|mizuya dōko]]'' Image:Youkoukan06n4592.jpg|Room with tatami flooring in an inauspicious layout and paper doors ([[:en:Shōji|Shōji]]) File:Anforet 3F 2017-06-02 ac (2).jpg|An auspicious layout File:ITatami (8465411258).jpg|"T" shape File:Tatami (51687126569).jpg|Ryūkyū ''tatami'' are square shaped without borders </gallery>
